rule: Firms must ensure that data used to develop and validate models is of sufficient quality, including completeness. Columns with excessive missing values undermine model reliability and statistical robustness.
flag: High missing data detected in columns (>20%)
suggestion: Investigate the root cause of missingness (MAR/MCAR/MNAR). Apply appropriate imputation or exclude columns exceeding the 20% threshold. Document the data quality assessment and remediation steps per SS1/23 Principle 3.2.

rule: Training data must adequately represent the population being modelled. Severe class imbalance (majority:minority ratio >10:1) can bias model outputs toward the majority class and result in poor minority-class detection.
flag: Severe class imbalance detected in target variable (ratio >10:1)
suggestion: Apply SMOTE, class weighting, or threshold tuning to address class imbalance. Document the chosen approach and quantify its impact on recall, precision, and PR-AUC per SS1/23 Principle 3.2.

rule: Duplicate records must be identified and removed prior to model training. Excessive duplicates (>5% of observations) introduce bias and violate the independence assumption between train and test observations.
flag: High proportion of duplicate records detected (>5%)
suggestion: Deduplicate the dataset before the train/test split. Investigate the source of duplicates and document the deduplication logic and impact on dataset size per SS1/23 Principle 3.2.

rule: Identifier columns (customer IDs, loan IDs, row numbers) must be excluded from model input features to prevent data leakage and ensure the model generalises to unseen borrowers.
flag: ID columns detected — risk of data leakage if included as model features
suggestion: Explicitly drop ID columns from the feature set prior to training. Confirm exclusion in the Model Development Document and pre-processing code review per SS1/23 Principle 3.2.

rule: The training dataset must contain a sufficient number of observations to support statistically robust model development. Fewer than 500 observations may produce unreliable parameter estimates and unstable model performance.
flag: Training dataset falls below minimum sample size of 500 observations
suggestion: Collect additional labelled observations or review model scope. If data is genuinely limited, apply rigorous cross-validation and document the known limitations per SS1/23 Principle 3.3.

rule: Features with very high pairwise correlation (>0.90) introduce multicollinearity, which can destabilise model coefficients, reduce interpretability, and lead to unstable SHAP attributions.
flag: Highly correlated feature pairs detected (correlation >0.90)
suggestion: Remove one feature from each highly correlated pair, or apply PCA/VIF-based dimensionality reduction. Document the feature selection decision and the retained features in the model log per SS1/23 Principle 3.3.

rule: Features with near-zero variance provide minimal discriminatory power, may negatively affect model stability, and should be reviewed before inclusion in the final feature set.
flag: Low-variance features identified that add minimal predictive value
suggestion: Review low-variance features for domain significance. If no business justification exists, remove them. Document the retention or exclusion rationale in the feature engineering log per SS1/23 Principle 3.3.

rule: Features with Information Value (IV) below 0.02 are classified as weak predictors. Their inclusion may introduce noise without improving discriminatory power.
flag: Features with low Information Value (<0.02) detected
suggestion: Review low-IV features for business relevance. Remove those without a business justification, retaining IV analysis and selection decisions in the feature engineering documentation per SS1/23 Principle 3.4.

rule: All models must be formally documented prior to deployment, including model type, purpose, key assumptions, hyperparameters, and known limitations.
flag: Model type not identified — model documentation may be incomplete
suggestion: Ensure the model name, architecture, hyperparameters, and training configuration are recorded in the Model Development Document (MDD) before submitting to validation per SS1/23 Principle 3.1.

rule: Firms should train and evaluate multiple candidate models (challenger models) to justify the selected approach and demonstrate it is the most appropriate for the intended use.
flag: No challenger model comparison performed
suggestion: Train at least one additional model (e.g. logistic regression as baseline, LightGBM as challenger) and document the comparison of their performance metrics. Justify the selected model per SS1/23 Principle 3.3.

rule: Cross-validation must be used to obtain an unbiased estimate of out-of-sample model performance and reduce the risk of overfitting to a single train/test split.
flag: Cross-validation was not used during model training
suggestion: Implement stratified k-fold cross-validation (k≥5 recommended). Report mean and standard deviation of ROC-AUC, recall, and PR-AUC across folds in the validation report per SS1/23 Principle 3.3.

rule: When synthetic oversampling techniques such as SMOTE are applied, the approach must be formally documented with justification, parameters, and evaluation of its impact on model performance.
flag: SMOTE applied — ensure rationale and parameters are formally documented
suggestion: Document the SMOTE parameters (k_neighbors, sampling_strategy), the pre/post class distributions, and the measured impact on recall, precision, and PR-AUC in the Model Development Document per SS1/23 Principle 3.5.

rule: Credit risk classification models must achieve a minimum ROC-AUC of 0.70 on the held-out test set to demonstrate acceptable discriminatory power for regulatory purposes.
flag: ROC-AUC below minimum threshold of 0.70
suggestion: Investigate feature quality, model selection, and hyperparameter tuning. Consider ensemble methods or additional predictive features. A model below 0.70 AUC should not proceed to deployment per SS1/23 Principle 4.1.

rule: The model must achieve a minimum recall of 0.60 on the defaulted class to ensure the majority of true credit risk events are identified before loan approval decisions are made.
flag: Recall (sensitivity) below minimum threshold of 0.60
suggestion: Adjust the classification threshold, retrain with class-weighted loss, or apply SMOTE to improve minority-class detection. A recall below 0.60 represents an unacceptable default miss-rate per SS1/23 Principle 4.4.

rule: The model must achieve a minimum precision of 0.40 on the predicted default class to control the rate of false-positive classifications and limit unjustified loan rejections.
flag: Precision below minimum threshold of 0.40
suggestion: Review the classification threshold and model calibration. Excessive false positives can result in unjustified credit denials. Document the business impact and calibration approach in the model report per SS1/23 Principle 4.4.

rule: The absolute gap between cross-validation mean AUC and held-out test AUC must not exceed 0.10, as larger gaps indicate potential overfitting or data leakage between train and test partitions.
flag: Large gap between CV AUC and test AUC — potential overfitting or data leakage
suggestion: Increase regularisation, reduce model complexity, or expand the training dataset. Audit the data pipeline for leakage between train and test partitions. Document the investigation and remediation per SS1/23 Principle 4.4.

rule: Model outputs must be explainable using model-agnostic interpretability techniques (e.g. SHAP, LIME) to support human oversight, credit decisions, and regulatory scrutiny.
flag: Model explainability analysis not performed
suggestion: Apply SHAP values to explain both global feature importance and individual loan-level predictions. Include explainability outputs and a summary narrative in the validation report per SS1/23 Principle 4.2.

rule: Any deviation from the default 0.5 classification threshold must be formally documented with a business justification, impact analysis, and sign-off from the model risk governance function.
flag: Non-default decision threshold requires formal documentation and governance sign-off
suggestion: Prepare a threshold justification note documenting the chosen value, the business rationale (e.g. asymmetric cost of default vs. false approval), and the precision/recall trade-off analysis per SS1/23 Principle 5.1.

rule: Residual heteroscedasticity in model errors may indicate systematic model bias across score bands or borrower segments and must be investigated and documented where detected.
flag: Potential heteroscedasticity detected in model residuals
suggestion: Investigate the source of heteroscedasticity by score band, loan size, and borrower segment. Apply variance-stabilising transformations or segment-specific calibration where necessary. Document findings per SS1/23 Principle 2.1.

rule: The Precision-Recall AUC (PR-AUC) must meet a minimum threshold of 0.30, as it is a more informative metric than ROC-AUC for imbalanced credit risk datasets.
flag: PR-AUC below minimum threshold of 0.30
suggestion: PR-AUC reflects model performance across all thresholds under class imbalance. Investigate model calibration, threshold optimisation, and feature enrichment to improve PR-AUC per SS1/23 Principle 3.3.

rule: IFRS 9 paragraph 5.5 requires that models used for Expected Credit Loss (ECL) estimation produce a calibrated Probability of Default (PD) output for each individual exposure.
flag: Model does not produce a Probability of Default (PD) output required for IFRS 9 ECL
suggestion: Ensure the model outputs a calibrated PD score in the range [0,1] for each borrower. Apply Platt scaling or isotonic regression for calibration. PD is a mandatory input to ECL = PD × LGD × EAD per IFRS 9.5.5.

rule: IFRS 9.5.5.3 requires that financial instruments be classified into Stage 1, Stage 2, or Stage 3 based on changes in credit risk since initial recognition, determining the ECL measurement horizon.
flag: Stage 1/2/3 classification logic not implemented
suggestion: Implement IFRS 9 staging: Stage 1 (12-month ECL, no significant deterioration), Stage 2 (lifetime ECL, significant increase in credit risk), Stage 3 (lifetime ECL, credit-impaired). Document staging criteria and thresholds per IFRS 9.5.5.3.

rule: IFRS 9.B5.5.49 requires that forward-looking macroeconomic information (e.g. GDP growth, unemployment rate, house price index) be incorporated into ECL models in an unbiased and probability-weighted manner.
flag: Forward-looking macroeconomic variables not detected in dataset
suggestion: Incorporate macroeconomic scenario variables (GDP growth, unemployment rate, interest rates, HPI) into the model. Apply at least three economic scenarios (base, upside, downside) and probability-weight the ECL outputs per IFRS 9.B5.5.49.

rule: IFRS 9.5.5.7 requires the identification and flagging of Significant Increases in Credit Risk (SICR) since origination, which triggers the transfer from Stage 1 to Stage 2 and a switch to lifetime ECL measurement.
flag: Significant Increase in Credit Risk (SICR) not flagged in model output
suggestion: Implement SICR detection using both quantitative triggers (e.g. PD doubling since origination, 30+ DPD) and qualitative triggers (e.g. watchlist status, forbearance). Document all SICR criteria and backstop provisions per IFRS 9.5.5.7.

rule: IFRS 9.5.5.3 requires that Lifetime Expected Credit Losses be estimated for financial instruments classified as Stage 2 or Stage 3, reflecting the risk of default over the remaining life of the instrument.
flag: Lifetime Expected Credit Loss (ECL) not estimated for Stage 2/3 exposures
suggestion: Implement ECL = PD(lifetime) × LGD × EAD for Stage 2 and Stage 3 exposures. Use forward-looking PD term structures rather than point-in-time 12-month PD. Incorporate scenario-weighted macroeconomic adjustments per IFRS 9.5.5.3.

rule: IFRS 7.34 requires disclosure of credit risk concentration analysis, covering exposures by geographic region, industry sector, and counterparty type.
flag: Credit risk concentration analysis not performed
suggestion: Perform and disclose concentration analysis by sector, geography, and counterparty. Identify top-10 single-name exposures and any concentration exceeding 10% of the portfolio. Present results in tabular form per IFRS 7.34.

rule: IFRS 7.36 requires disclosure of the maximum exposure to credit risk for each class of financial instrument, stated gross before any collateral held or credit enhancements.
flag: Maximum credit risk exposure (EAD) not reported by portfolio segment
suggestion: Calculate and disclose the gross maximum credit exposure (EAD) by product type and portfolio segment, without netting collateral or credit enhancements. Present as a tabular disclosure in the model output per IFRS 7.36.

rule: IFRS 7.37 requires an ageing analysis of financial assets that are past due but not impaired, and a separate breakdown of individually impaired financial assets.
flag: Past due and impaired asset breakdown not provided
suggestion: Produce an ageing schedule segmenting exposures into: current, 1-30 DPD, 31-60 DPD, 61-90 DPD, and 90+ DPD (default). Include a separate breakdown for impaired vs. past-due-not-impaired assets per IFRS 7.37.
